candidate_instructions.patient: A 34-year-old woman has presented for assessment
candidate_instructions.note: At the end of the station, the examiner may ask you some further questions

abdominal_1.description: Patient presenting to emergency department with acute epigastric pain, likely acute pancreatitis
abdominal_1.symptoms: Epigastric pain that started suddenly 90 minutes ago, sharp and gripping, severity 11 out of 10, radiates through to the back, constant and worsening, worse on deep inspiration and movement, relieved slightly by leaning forward, associated nausea and fever, feeling breathless even when sitting down, bowels opened normally this morning, 3kg intentional weight loss over last 4 weeks due to new fitness regime
abdominal_1.negative_features: No jaundice, no dysphagia, no reflux, no change in bowel habits, no blood in stool, no confusion, no recent travel, no night sweats
abdominal_1.past_medical_history: Gallstones a few months ago but this pain is worse, gestational diabetes during both pregnancies but resolved
abdominal_1.drug_history: Combined oral contraceptive pill Gedarel 30/150, no known drug allergies
abdominal_1.family_history: Mother had gallstones and type 2 diabetes
abdominal_1.social_history: Lives with husband and two children aged 5 and 1, full-time mother, non-smoker, drinks 2 bottles of wine on weekends only, recently started running and new diet

ice.ideas: I don't know what's going on, maybe a stomach bug?
ice.expectations: I want the pain to stop. Please help me!

viva_questions.question: What further clinical examinations or assessments would you like to perform?
Describe some appropriate further investigations for this patient.

marking_points.point: Bloods including FBC, U&Es, LFTs, CRP, amylase

viva_questions.question: What is your differential diagnosis? Please give your most likely diagnosis and two other possibilities.
Describe the management of acute pancreatitis.

marking_points.point: Monitoring including urine output and NEWS score

viva_questions.question: What is the definitive management for gallstone pancreatitis?

marking_points.point: Urgent ERCP and laparoscopic cholecystectomy before discharge

candidate_instructions.role: medical student on placement in the surgical assessment unit
candidate_instructions.patient: A 59-year-old woman has presented for assessment
candidate_instructions.note: At the end of the station, the examiner may ask you some further questions

abdominal_2.description: Patient presenting to surgical assessment unit with severe crampy abdominal pain, likely bowel obstruction secondary to Crohn's disease flare
abdominal_2.symptoms: Severe crampy abdominal pain since 3am, woken up by it, pain comes and goes every 5 minutes, generalised around centre and lower abdomen, severity 10 out of 10, gradually worsening over past 2 days starting as bloating, 5 episodes of vomiting this morning with bright green then dark brown offensive smelling vomit, abdomen feels rock hard and distended, one episode of diarrhoea with blood in last 48 hours, still passing some wind, tried paracetamol and ibuprofen but vomited them up
abdominal_2.negative_features: No fever, no joint pain, no numbness or tingling in legs, no blood in vomit, no chest pain, no left arm pain
abdominal_2.past_medical_history: Crohn's disease flaring recently treated with prednisolone and azathioprine, last menstrual period 9 years ago, 2 caesarean sections 25 and 27 years ago
abdominal_2.drug_history: Prednisolone, azathioprine, hormone replacement therapy with oestrogen patch and Mirena coil, no known drug allergies
abdominal_2.family_history: Grandmother had Crohn's disease, everyone else at home is well
abdominal_2.social_history: Lives at home with husband, 2 sons live in London, never smoked, drinks less than half a bottle of wine a week, no recreational drugs, plays tennis once a week and enjoys long walks with dog, works as head teacher which is stressful

ice.ideas: I'm worried it is something to do with my Crohn's disease
ice.concerns: I have a friend with Crohn's who has a stoma and I'm very worried I will need one now
ice.expectations: I would like some pain relief and something to stop me feeling so sick

abdominal_2.correct_diagnosis: Bowel obstruction secondary to Crohn's disease flare

candidate_instructions.role: medical student working in general practice
candidate_instructions.patient: A 52-year-old man has presented with abdominal pain
candidate_instructions.note: At the end of the station, the examiner may ask you some further questions

abdominal_3.description: Patient presenting to general practice with intermittent epigastric pain, likely GERD or peptic ulcer disease
abdominal_3.symptoms: Intermittent burning pain in epigastric region for 4 months, severity 5 to 6 out of 10, occurring 2 to 3 times daily, worst in evenings after dinner and when lying down, associated bloating belching and acid reflux with horrible taste in mouth, worse after meals when lying down and when using NSAIDs, antacids helped initially but no longer working, ibuprofen and paracetamol made it worse, mornings are usually fine
abdominal_3.negative_features: No jaundice, no ulcers, no vomiting, no blood in vomit, no dysphagia, no difficulty swallowing, no abdominal distension, no constipation or diarrhoea, no blood in stool, no melaena, no PR bleeding, no shortness of breath, no palpitations
abdominal_3.past_medical_history: Hypertension well controlled on medication, no history of heart disease or angina, no abdominal disease, no previous surgeries
abdominal_3.drug_history: Ramipril 2.5mg daily, occasional over the counter paracetamol which has not helped, no allergies
abdominal_3.family_history: Father has stable angina, mother has Alzheimer's disease in nursing home, no family history of gastrointestinal disease
abdominal_3.social_history: Lives in semi-detached home with wife and 2 children, full-time solicitor with very stressful job trying to become partner, infrequent smoker a few cigarettes a week when stressed, drinks alcohol a few times a week about one bottle of wine over the weekend, not exercising, poor diet snacking at work and big meals late at night, drinks a lot of caffeine, no recreational drugs

ice.ideas: I'm worried it could be pain from my heart
ice.concerns: I've put on weight recently and work is stressful, worried it could be angina like my dad
ice.expectations: I was hoping you could do a blood test or a scan to check there is nothing wrong with my heart

abdominal_3.correct_diagnosis: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease (GERD) or peptic ulcer disease

What are the most appropriate investigations for this patient?

marking_points.point: Bloods including FBC, U&Es, LFTs, CRP, amylase

viva_questions.question: What are the differential diagnoses and which is most likely?
What is the most appropriate management for this patient?

marking_points.point: Conservative — lifestyle changes including reducing coffee alcohol stopping smoking smaller meals avoid eating before bedtime avoid triggers
Medical — proton pump inhibitors such as omeprazole or lansoprazole
Medical — H2RA therapy if PPI inadequate
Surgical — laparoscopic fundoplication if not wishing to remain on long term PPI

candidate_instructions.role: medical student working in the emergency department
candidate_instructions.patient: A 55-year-old man has presented with chest pain
candidate_instructions.note: At the end of the station, the examiner may ask you some further questions

chest_1.description: Patient presenting to emergency department with severe central chest pain, likely aortic dissection
chest_1.symptoms: Severe central chest pain that came on suddenly 4 hours ago at work, sharp and fierce ripping pain in the centre, severity 7 out of 10, radiates straight through to between the shoulder blades, started at maximal intensity within seconds and has not eased off, nothing makes it better or worse, breathing in does not change it, not better lying flat or leaning forward
chest_1.negative_features: No chest or back trauma, no sweating, no shortness of breath, no palpitations, no syncope, no abdominal pain, no rashes, no cough, no haemoptysis, no nausea or vomiting, no fever, no reflux or heartburn, no recent travel
chest_1.past_medical_history: Hypertension poorly controlled, keeps changing tablets and misses doses when busy, no history of heart disease or angina, no previous surgeries
chest_1.drug_history: Losartan 100mg daily, amlodipine 10mg daily, indapamide 2.5mg daily, occasional ibuprofen and paracetamol for work injuries, amlodipine causes slight ankle swelling, no known allergies
chest_1.family_history: Father died of dementia aged 80, mother died of stroke aged 77, no family history of cardiovascular disease
chest_1.social_history: Lives in rented house with partner, self-employed builder working on building sites since leaving school, ex-smoker with 10 pack year history quit 5 years ago, minimal alcohol one or two pints a month, active at work but no formal exercise, poor diet at work mostly takeaways but trying to cook fresh meals at home with partner, previous cocaine use once or twice in late twenties but nothing since, drives a van for work

ice.ideas: I'm worried it could be a heart attack
ice.concerns: My friend died of a heart attack at 58 and he had bad chest pains too
ice.expectations: I want to know what this is. If it is a heart attack do I need a stent or something like that?

viva_questions.question: Describe the initial management of a confirmed aortic dissection.

marking_points.point: Control of blood pressure with IV labetalol

viva_questions.question: Describe the surgical management of aortic dissection.

marking_points.point: Type A dissections require open surgery to prevent aortic rupture
Type B dissections are usually managed medically with endovascular intervention for complicated cases

candidate_instructions.role: junior doctor completing a rotation in general practice
candidate_instructions.patient: A 55-year-old man has presented with chest pain
candidate_instructions.note: At the end of the station, the examiner may ask you some further questions

chest_2.description: Patient presenting to general practice with recurring chest pain on exertion, likely stable angina
chest_2.symptoms: Central chest pain occurring a couple of times per week for the last 3 months, tight feeling like a band squeezing across the chest, severity 5 out of 10, radiates into neck on both sides and sometimes left arm, associated shortness of breath at the same time as chest pain, pain is not worse on deep breathing, each episode lasts a few minutes, comes on suddenly during physical exertion at work especially carrying heavy things up stairs, goes away completely with rest within a few minutes
chest_2.negative_features: No syncope or collapse, no palpitations, no breathlessness when lying flat, no waking up gasping at night, no cough, no blood in sputum, no reflux, no weight loss, otherwise well in himself
chest_2.past_medical_history: Hypertension diagnosed aged 50 on medication, high cholesterol diagnosed shortly after hypertension
chest_2.drug_history: Ramipril, amlodipine, atorvastatin which he does not take because his cousin had shoulder aches from it and he cannot afford that with his physical job, allergic to penicillin which gives him diarrhoea
chest_2.family_history: Younger brother had heart attack aged 50 treated with a stent, father died of heart attack aged 75 and had hypertension and high cholesterol, two brothers and father had type 2 diabetes, mother had a stroke aged 70
chest_2.social_history: Lives with wife and two daughters studying A-levels so stressful household, builder mostly on big commercial jobs, stopped smoking 3 months ago when chest pains started previously smoked 20 a day for 30 years, shares a few bottles of wine a week with wife, no illicit drugs, physically active at work on feet all day, diet could be better toast for breakfast sandwich for lunch and home cooked dinner with snacks, BMI 30 borderline overweight

ice.ideas: I thought it was a pulled muscle at first but it has been going on too long
ice.concerns: I'm worried it is something serious like a heart attack
ice.expectations: I hope you can give me the all clear so I can get back to work

What medication should be offered for rapid symptom relief of angina?
Describe how the patient should use GTN spray.

marking_points.point: Stop activity and take one dose of GTN when attack occurs
Must sit down due to risk of lightheadedness and hypotension
Take second dose after five minutes if pain not eased
Call ambulance if pain not eased five minutes after second dose or if pain intensifies or patient unwell

candidate_instructions.role: junior doctor completing a rotation in the emergency department
candidate_instructions.patient: A 50-year-old man has presented with chest pain
candidate_instructions.note: At the end of the station, the examiner may ask you some further questions

chest_3.description: Patient presenting to emergency department with severe left sided chest pain, likely ST-elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI)
chest_3.symptoms: Left sided chest pain that came on suddenly out of nowhere, woke him up at 6am and wife called ambulance immediately, heavy tight pressing feeling against the chest, severity 9 out of 10, constant and has not changed since it started, radiates up into neck and jaw and down left arm, associated nausea but not vomited yet, feeling clammy and sweaty, nothing makes it better or worse, does not worsen when sitting standing or lying down, not worse on deep breathing
chest_3.negative_features: No shortness of breath, no syncope or collapse, no fatigue, no palpitations, no previous episodes like this
chest_3.past_medical_history: Hypertension diagnosed about a year ago on medication, high cholesterol trying to improve diet with more fruit and vegetables
chest_3.drug_history: Ramipril, atorvastatin, no known drug allergies
chest_3.family_history: Sister had heart attack aged 50 treated with a stent, father died of heart attack aged 51, mother died of stroke aged 60
chest_3.social_history: Lives with wife and teenage son, engineer mostly doing project management now, shares a bottle of wine every evening with wife, non-smoker, no illicit drugs, diet is coffee for breakfast sandwich with 4 slices of bread for lunch and often fish for dinner, walks to work when weather is ok about 40 minutes each way

ice.ideas: I think I'm having a heart attack
ice.concerns: I'm concerned I won't make it
ice.expectations: I need something for the pain

marking_points.point: Primary percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) to open the vessel as quickly as possible

candidate_instructions.role: junior doctor completing a rotation in general practice
candidate_instructions.patient: A 24-year-old woman has presented for assessment
candidate_instructions.note: At the end of the station, the examiner may ask you some further questions

cough_1.description: Patient presenting to general practice with 4 week history of persistent cough following lower respiratory tract infection, likely post-infective cough
cough_1.symptoms: Persistent non-productive cough for 4 weeks throughout the day at home and at work, started 4 weeks ago with lower respiratory tract infection symptoms including productive cough with horrible phlegm and fever, treated with oral penicillin 2 weeks ago and felt much better since but cough has not fully resolved, antibiotics helped but cough still lingering, COVID-19 negative on 2 PCR tests, feels well in herself otherwise apart from the cough
cough_1.negative_features: No haemoptysis, no chest pain, no breathlessness, no wheeze, no post-nasal drip, no heartburn or reflux, no hoarseness, no weight loss, no current fever, no night sweats, no specific symptoms of systemic disease, no recent overseas travel
cough_1.past_medical_history: No history of pre-existing respiratory disease, childhood immunisations up to date, has had 3 COVID vaccinations, no surgical history
cough_1.drug_history: No regular prescribed medication, tried Benylin cough syrup from supermarket which did not help much, no known allergies
cough_1.family_history: Father has hypertension on medication, no other significant family history
cough_1.social_history: Lives with parents, works as a waitress in a bar, occasional social smoker 2 to 3 cigarettes on a night out once a week since age 18, moderate alcohol goes out once a week with friends and drinks about a bottle and a half of wine, tried cannabis once did not like it, parents have a cat at home

ice.ideas: I don't think I have cleared the infection, could you give me some more antibiotics please
ice.concerns: It is a bit embarrassing at work because I am coughing all the time and people think I have COVID but all my tests have been negative
ice.expectations: Maybe I need some antibiotics in case it is an infection which has not gone away

cough_1.correct_diagnosis: Post-infective cough following resolved lower respiratory tract infection

marking_points.point: Post-infectious cough is usually self-limiting but may last up to 8 weeks
If not improving after 8 weeks attend for re-review

candidate_instructions.role: medical student working in general practice
candidate_instructions.patient: A 64-year-old man has presented with a cough
candidate_instructions.note: At the end of the station, the examiner may ask you some further questions

cough_2.description: Patient presenting to general practice with 5 month history of persistent cough, breathlessness and haemoptysis, likely lung cancer given red flag symptoms and smoking history
cough_2.symptoms: Persistent cough for 5 months starting suddenly, initially thought it was an infection as husband was also unwell, cough started with small amount of green phlegm for a few days but no phlegm since, cough is becoming more frequent throughout the day, nothing triggers it and nothing makes it go away, noticed small amount of bright red blood on tissue last week which is what prompted the visit today, feeling fatigued and worn out after activities that previously were fine, trousers are falling down suggesting unintentional weight loss but no weighing scale at home, breathlessness on exertion only over the last few weeks, used to be very fit but now gets short of breath walking up stairs quickly or running, breathlessness is stable and not getting considerably worse, no change overnight or in the morning
cough_2.negative_features: No chest pain, no wheeze, no breathlessness at rest or at night, no hoarseness, no voice changes, no changes to appetite, no difficulty swallowing, no urinary or bowel symptoms, no fever, no recent travel, no bird or animal exposure
cough_2.past_medical_history: Hypertension on medication, hypercholesterolaemia on statin, complete vaccination history including annual flu vaccine, due pneumonia vaccine at 65
cough_2.drug_history: Amlodipine 5mg daily for blood pressure, atorvastatin 20mg nightly for cholesterol, penicillin allergy causing anaphylaxis to amoxicillin so needs alternative antibiotics
cough_2.family_history: Both parents have hypertension on similar medication, father has Alzheimer's disease living in nursing home, wants to visit father but worried about spreading infection
cough_2.social_history: Lives with husband in owned house, main carer for mother who lives alone since father moved to nursing home, breathlessness making it harder to care for mother, retired architect for 40 years with potential asbestos exposure in buildings being demolished only mention if directly asked, drinks one bottle of wine on weekends, ex-smoker with 20 pack year history smoked 20 a day through twenties and thirties quit on fortieth birthday, no recreational drugs, used to walk a lot but now struggles to walk up hills without breathlessness, no recent travel, no bird or animal exposure

ice.ideas: I think it is the cold lingering, I have read that people can feel breathless and tired for up to 6 months after a bad cold
ice.concerns: I want to know how much longer this will go on, I want to get back to doing the things I used to enjoy
ice.expectations: Can you give me some antibiotics to help clear the infection quicker?

cough_2.correct_diagnosis: Lung cancer — red flags include haemoptysis, unintentional weight loss, fatigue, persistent cough over 3 weeks, smoking history and asbestos exposure

history_of_complaint_cough.item: Asks when cough started and if sudden or gradual
Asks patient to describe cough and changes over time
Asks about specific characteristics including productive or non-productive sputum colour and volume
Asks about other symptoms associated with cough including infective symptoms and shortness of breath
Identifies red flags including night sweats hoarseness pleuritic chest pain and leg swelling
Screens for other key respiratory symptoms including wheeze chest pain and systemic symptoms
Clarifies how cough changed over time or throughout the day
Asks if patient has experienced repeated episodes of cough
Asks if anything makes cough better or worse

history_of_complaint_dyspnoea.item: Clarifies when dyspnoea started and if sudden or gradual
Asks if dyspnoea occurs on exertion or at rest
Asks how dyspnoea changed over time and identifies diurnal variation or PND
Asks if anything makes dyspnoea better or worse and identifies triggers
Establishes severity and how it affects activities

past_medical_history.item: Asks if patient has any medical conditions
Asks about pre-existing respiratory disease
Asks about immunisations against respiratory disease
Asks if patient has previously undergone surgeries or procedures

drug_history.item: Asks about prescribed medications and OTC remedies
Asks about allergies and reaction type

family_history.item: Asks about family history of respiratory disease
Asks about family history of any relevant medical conditions

social_history.item: Asks about current and previous occupations including exposure to pulmonary irritants or asbestos
Asks about pets and hobbies associated with respiratory disease

closing_consultation.item: Summarises the key points
Asks patient if they have any questions or concerns not addressed
Thanks the patient for their time
Disposes PPE and washes hands

viva_questions.question: What further clinical examinations or assessments would you like to perform?
Describe some appropriate further investigations for this patient.

marking_points.point: Bedside — sputum culture or early morning sputum for acid-fast bacilli
Bloods including FBC, U&Es, LFTs, bone profile
Imaging — chest X-ray and CT thorax abdomen pelvis for staging
Bronchoscopy and biopsy or endobronchial ultrasound or PET-CT

viva_questions.question: What are the differential diagnoses and which is most likely?
What is the most appropriate management for lung cancer?

marking_points.point: Conservative — supportive therapies and palliative care

candidate_instructions.role: medical student working in general practice
candidate_instructions.patient: A 56-year-old man has presented with a cough
candidate_instructions.note: At the end of the station, the examiner may ask you some further questions

cough_3.description: Patient presenting to general practice with several months of productive cough, breathlessness, pleuritic chest pain, weight loss and night sweats, likely pneumonia or serious respiratory infection
cough_3.symptoms: Productive chesty cough for several months, started as dry cough and progressed to bringing up green and yellow phlegm, no bursts or coughing fits, bloody phlegm noticed in last couple of weeks only if directly asked, shorter of breath than usual needing to take a break climbing the hill to his house, pleuritic chest pain with sharp pain when coughing heavily or taking a deep breath, family have commented he looks thin and he has had to tighten his belt suggesting unintentional weight loss, reduced appetite lately, feels warm and sometimes very hot and sweaty overnight with night sweats, generally feels quite unwell and not himself
cough_3.negative_features: No urinary symptoms, no nasal blockage or runny nose, no wheeze, no confusion or neurological symptoms, no joint pain, no rashes or skin changes, no recent travel, no unwell contacts
cough_3.past_medical_history: Type 2 diabetes diagnosed a few years ago, asthma with several courses of steroids this year including one hospital admission treated with nebulisers, fully vaccinated against flu and covid
cough_3.drug_history: Metformin 1g twice daily, gliclazide 80mg once daily, salbutamol blue reliever inhaler as needed which does not help much with current cough, budesonide 200 micrograms preventer inhaler once daily, prednisolone 30mg taken 3 courses in last 12 months, paracetamol to help feel better and bring down temperature, no known allergies
cough_3.family_history: Father has asthma, mother has hypertension but otherwise in good health
cough_3.social_history: Lives with wife, senior healthcare assistant working on gastroenterology ward at a hospital, one to two beers a week, non-smoker never smoked, tried LSD in younger years but nothing recently

ice.ideas: I don't know what the cause is, isn't that your job
ice.concerns: I'm worried it could be something serious like lung cancer
ice.expectations: I'm expecting to be told to come back if I'm no better in a few weeks, that's what you usually tell me

cough_3.correct_diagnosis: Pneumonia — key features include productive cough with green sputum, haemoptysis, pleuritic chest pain, night sweats, weight loss, fever and immunocompromised state from diabetes and steroids

opening_consultation.item: Washes hands and dons PPE if appropriate
Introduces themselves including name and role
Confirms patient's name and date of birth
Explains they'd like to take a history
Gains consent to proceed with history taking

presenting_complaint.item: Uses open questioning to explore presenting complaint

history_of_complaint.item: Character: establishes that the cough feels chesty
Timing: clarifies when cough started and if getting worse
Sputum: establishes colour smell and amount
Haemoptysis: asks about presence of blood in sputum
Chest pain
Dyspnoea
Fevers and rigors
Muscle aches
Urinary symptoms
Weight loss
Neurological symptoms
Time course: asks how symptoms changed over time
Exacerbating or relieving factors
Explores ICE
Summarises presenting complaint

systems_review.item: Screens for relevant symptoms in other body systems

past_medical_history.item: Asks about respiratory conditions
Asks about recent hospital admissions or surgical procedures

drug_history.item: Asks about prescribed medications and OTC remedies
Asks about allergies and reaction type
Asks about immunisations against respiratory diseases

family_history.item: Asks about family history of respiratory conditions and malignancy

social_history.item: Asks about recent travel to areas of high TB prevalence

closing_consultation.item: Summarises salient points and asks if anything missed
Thanks the patient for their time
Disposes PPE and washes hands

viva_questions.question: What further clinical examinations or assessments would you like to perform?
Describe some appropriate further investigations for this patient.

marking_points.point: Bloods including FBC, U&E, CRP, viral screen, consider BNP
Sputum microscopy culture and sensitivities with early morning samples

viva_questions.question: What is your differential diagnosis? Please give your most likely diagnosis and two other possibilities.
What measures should be taken to reduce the transmission of TB?

marking_points.point: Screening should be offered to close contacts
Inpatients with suspected TB should be managed in single room or negative pressure room

viva_questions.question: What is the treatment course for active TB without CNS involvement?

marking_points.point: Antibiotic therapy with isoniazid rifampicin pyrazinamide and ethambutol for two months
Followed by isoniazid and rifampicin for a further four months

viva_questions.question: Name two factors that should be monitored during antibiotic treatment of TB.

marking_points.point: Liver function due to rifampicin and isoniazid hepatotoxicity
Visual acuity due to ethambutol causing visual disturbances
